narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 37 and a middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways but faced challenges from your earlier academic record. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 61.5% chance that you would fail it—above the typical threshold for approval, leading to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your performance rankings in school, specifically your Year 3 decile of 2, which placed you in the bottom 20% of your peers compared to the average of around the 5th or 6th decile. That lower Year 3 ranking was the single biggest factor pushing the model toward predicting bar failure, as it signaled potential struggles in sustaining strong performance over time. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also below the average of about 6th, reinforced this concern by highlighting an early pattern of grades that didn't keep pace with most students. On a brighter note, your LSAT score was the most influential positive factor, slightly above the average of 37, which pulled the prediction back toward success and showed your aptitude for the kind of reasoning the bar demands. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 trailed the typical 3.21, and your plan to attend full-time aligned with the vast majority of students, these played smaller roles compared to your deciles and LSAT. Overall, your profile compared to typical admitted students by having stronger test-taking potential but weaker sustained academic deciles, tipping the balance against you. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes bar passage stats to protect the school's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate or targeted GPA improvement, and reapply with that evidence. You might also seek feedback from admissions counselors on bolstering your narrative around these metrics. With your LSAT strength as a foundation, targeted improvements could flip this prediction in your favor next time. Keep pushing; law needs determined voices like yours.
